slides.title: What a class really is
body: A class is a BLUEPRINT. It says 'here is what a thing of this type looks like — what data it holds and what it can do.'
Real example: Tesla has one Model 3 blueprint. Every Model 3 ever built follows that blueprint. Each individual car is an OBJECT (an instance) — same shape, but with its own VIN, mileage, and owner.

vocabulary.def: The class definition — the spec for what every instance of this class looks like.
One actual object built from a class.

slides.title: Why classes matter for Salesforce
body: Every Salesforce SObject — Account, Contact, Opportunity — is just a class Salesforce defined for you. You access fields and methods on those objects exactly the same way you'll access your own.
Real example: Account.Name, Contact.Email, Opportunity.Amount — those are all properties on Salesforce-defined classes. The difference between Salesforce's classes and yours is just who wrote them.

slides.title: Your first class
slides.intro: Type this. The simplest possible class — just data, no behavior yet.
lineExplanations: 'public class Car' — defining a class named Car that any other class can use. The { opens the class body.
Declare a property: every Car instance has its own 'model' String. 'public' means anyone can read/write it. The semicolon ends the property declaration.
Another property: 'year' as an Integer. The } below closes the class. That's it — a class with two pieces of data.

slides.title: Constructing an instance
body: The 'new' keyword creates an instance from a class. After 'new ClassName()', you have a real object you can read from and write to.
Real example: 'new HttpRequest()' is how every Apex callout to Stripe or Twilio starts. Instantiate, configure, send.

vocabulary.def: Apex keyword that creates a new instance of a class.

slides.title: Creating and using a Car
slides.intro: Type this. Construct a Car, set its properties, read them back.
lineExplanations: 'new Car()' creates a fresh instance — both properties start as null. Assign the instance to a variable named myCar of type Car.
'myCar.model' = the model property OF this specific car. Object dot property = standard syntax everywhere in Apex.
Set the year. Each Car instance has its OWN model and year — independent of every other Car ever created.
Read both properties and concatenate. Prints 'Model 3 2024'.

slides.title: Methods — what a class can DO
body: Methods are functions defined inside a class. They describe behavior — what the object can do, or what the class as a whole can compute.
Real example: Apex's Datetime.now() is a method. The List class has methods .add(), .size(), .clear(). Every method you'll ever write follows the same shape.

vocabulary.def: A named, reusable block of code defined inside a class.

slides.title: Adding a method
slides.intro: Type this. Now Car can DO something — describe itself.
lineExplanations: Same class declaration as before.
model and year — same two properties.
Method shape: public (anyone can call), String (returns a String), describe (the name), () (no parameters), { (opens the body).
Inside the method: 'return' produces a value back to whoever called the method. We concatenate year and model and return that String.
} closes the method body. } closes the class. Two opens, two closes — always match them.

slides.title: Calling a method on an instance
slides.intro: Type this. Once an object has a method, you call it with object.method().
lineExplanations: Construct.
Set model.
Set year.
Call myCar.describe(). The method runs against THIS specific car and returns a String. Assign that to a variable.
Prints '2024 Model 3'.

slides.title: Constructors — initial state
body: A CONSTRUCTOR is a special method that runs automatically when you 'new' an instance. Its job: set up starting values so you don't have to.
Real example: when Stripe's API returns a Charge object, its constructor stamps the timestamp, generates an Id, and sets status='pending' — all before the object reaches your code.

vocabulary.def: Special method that runs when an instance is created. Same name as the class. No return type.

slides.title: A class with a constructor
slides.intro: Type this. The constructor takes parameters and stores them on the new instance.
lineExplanations: Class declaration.
Two properties.
Constructor: same name as the class (Car). No return type — not even void. Takes two parameters: m (String) and y (Integer).
Inside the constructor: assign the parameter m to the property model.
Assign y to year. Two } below close constructor and class.

slides.title: Constructing with arguments
slides.intro: Type this. Now constructing a Car is one line — no separate property assignments needed.
lineExplanations: new Car('Model 3', 2024) — pass arguments to the constructor. The constructor runs and sets the properties for us. One line replaces three.
Prints 'Model 3'.
Prints 2024. Both properties were set by the constructor.

slides.front: What's the special thing about a constructor?
slides.back: It has the SAME NAME as the class, no return type, and runs automatically when you write 'new ClassName(...)'.
slides.detail: You don't call constructors directly. Apex calls them for you when you use 'new'.

slides.title: Static — belongs to the CLASS, not an instance
body: A 'static' method or property belongs to the class itself, not to any one instance. Call it as ClassName.method() — no 'new' needed.

Real example: Datetime.now() is static — there's no specific Datetime instance you call it on. UserInfo.getUserId() is static — one call, returns the current user. Compare with myCar.describe() — that's instance-level.

vocabulary.def: Modifier meaning 'belongs to the class itself, not any specific instance.'

slides.title: A static utility method
slides.intro: Type this. Math is a class. PI is a static property. abs() is a static method. No instance needed.
lineExplanations: Math.PI is a static property. Use it directly via ClassName.property — no 'new Math()'. Same shape every time.
Math.abs(-42) is a static method call. Returns 42. The class itself does the work.
Prints ~31.4 (PI * 10).
Prints 42. abs (absolute value) strips the negative sign.

slides.front: When should a method be static instead of instance?
slides.back: When it doesn't depend on data inside any specific instance — it's a pure utility (like Math.abs) or it operates on the class as a whole (like 'how many cars total were manufactured').
slides.detail: Rule of thumb: if your method body would never use 'this.something', make it static.

slides.title: Constants — public static final
body: A 'public static final' property is a constant — a value that can never change after it's set. Convention: name it in ALL_CAPS with underscores.
Real example: Apex's StatusCode class has constants like StatusCode.REQUIRED_FIELD_MISSING. Salesforce uses constants everywhere to avoid 'magic strings' scattered across the codebase.

vocabulary.def: A named value that can never change after it's defined. Apex syntax: public static final TYPE NAME = value;
Modifier meaning 'this value cannot be reassigned.'

slides.title: Defining constants
slides.intro: Type this. Constants live at the top of a class, capitalized, never reassigned.
lineExplanations: Class declaration.
A public static final String. Name in ALL_CAPS by convention. The value 'Red' is locked — assigning to COLOR_RED later would fail to compile.
Another constant.
Integer constants work the same way. Reading: CarFactory.MAX_DOORS gives 4 — anywhere in the codebase.

slides.question: You want a method that returns the current time, with no specific instance involved. Should it be static or instance?
options: Instance — every Datetime object has its own 'now'.
Static — there's no 'specific instance' to call it on; it's a utility.
slides.explanation: That's exactly why Datetime.now() is static. It belongs to the Datetime class, not to any specific Datetime instance.

slides.title: Putting it together
body: Every Apex class you'll meet — Salesforce-provided or your own — uses these same building blocks: properties, constructors, instance methods, static methods, constants.

Real example: Salesforce's Account class has Name and AnnualRevenue properties, a constructor (called by SOQL), instance methods like .clone(), and is referenced via static lookups in your code. Same shapes you just typed.

slides.title: What you now know
body: You can define a class with properties, methods, a constructor, static utilities, and constants. You can construct instances and call methods on them.
Next lessons: Loops, Methods deep-dive, then Triggers — where every concept from Lessons 0–3 finally clicks together in production-grade Apex.
